statusLine: EU fuel security remains under pressure with 20 of 27 countries holding critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, though overall reserves stay above emergency thresholds.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security shows mixed signals in March 2026, with aggregate reserves remaining above the 90-day mandatory minimum but significant disparities across countries and fuel types. EU-wide averages stand at 96.3 days for petrol, 88.8 days for diesel, and 87.3 days for jet fuel. While petrol stocks maintain a reasonable buffer above the emergency threshold, diesel and jet fuel reserves are running tight at just under 90 days.

The distribution of risk is highly uneven across the 27 EU member states. Luxembourg faces the most severe challenges with critical stock levels across all three fuel types, holding just 38 days of petrol reserves. Poland, Austria, and Romania also show critical status in multiple fuel categories. At the other extreme, countries like Estonia, Latvia, and Ireland maintain robust reserves well above minimum requirements, with Estonia holding 320 days of jet fuel stocks.

Fuel pricing reflects underlying supply pressures, with Brent crude at $86.43 per barrel representing a significant 19.2% increase since late February's Middle East supply disruptions. Average EU petrol prices have reached €1.786 per liter, with notable variations from Malta's €1.34 to Denmark's €2.392. The recent strategic reserve releases by several countries have helped maintain minimum stock levels but highlight the ongoing strain on European fuel security.
keyPoints: 20 of 27 EU countries show critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, with Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania facing the most severe shortages
Diesel stocks at 88.8 days and jet fuel at 87.3 days are running dangerously close to the 90-day mandatory minimum threshold
Brent crude oil has surged 19.2% since late February's Middle East escalation, reaching $86.43 per barrel and driving up consumer fuel prices
Wide disparities exist between countries, with Estonia holding 320 days of jet fuel while Portugal has just 21.2 days
Strategic reserve releases have been necessary to maintain minimum stock requirements, indicating system stress
